meta.title: nono
meta.subject: nono — a capability-based sandbox runtime for AI agents

scenes.title: nono
scenes.tagline: A capability-based sandbox runtime for AI agents
scenes.footnote: Rust   ·   a four-crate workspace   ·   reviewed past the happy path

beats.narration: This is nono -- a runtime for letting an AI agent loose inside a real development environment without handing it the whole machine.

The idea: the agent gets only the host resources it actually needs -- specific paths, specific network destinations, specific credentials -- and a kernel enforces the rest. It sits deliberately between running the agent with full access to your keys and files, and sealing it inside a separate guest operating system.
It is written in Rust -- about a hundred and eight thousand lines, across four crates. We traced all of them, and this time, deliberately, past the happy path, into where it strains.

scenes.heading: Mechanism in the library, policy in the CLI

beats.narration: nono is a Cargo workspace of four crates -- and the single most important decision in the whole system is the line drawn between two of them.

At the centre, the library, just called nono. It is a pure sandbox primitive. It applies exactly the access a caller hands it -- and not one path more. It contains no list of dangerous commands, no list of sensitive files, no notion of what is safe. It is mechanism, with no policy at all.

Every security judgement instead lives one crate up, in nono-cli: the profiles, the deny rules, the dangerous-command lists. The library never decides; the command-line tool always does. That is why the same primitive can back the Python, TypeScript, and Go bindings -- none of them re-argue policy.
The proxy is the fourth crate -- it handles the network and credentials, and we will come back to it, because it is the part that worried us most.

And underneath everything, the kernel. nono does not invent isolation -- it drives Landlock on Linux and Seatbelt on macOS. The honesty of that is worth holding on to: nono is only ever as strong as the kernel beneath it.

scenes.heading: You grant; you never un-grant

beats.narration: Here is the library used directly. You build a CapabilitySet -- a list of explicit grants -- and then you apply it. This is the whole model.

Notice line four. Even slash-user-r, the system directory every program needs to start, has to be granted by hand. The library ships with nothing. Forget it, and your sandboxed process simply cannot run. That is the allow-list discipline: nothing is implied.

Read and write are separate grants, never one permission -- a slash-project that is read-write is a different, more dangerous thing than one that is read-only. And every path is canonicalized the instant it is granted, resolving symlinks up front so a later swap cannot smuggle access somewhere else.

Then line eight, and the word that defines nono. Apply is irreversible. Landlock's restrict-self, Seatbelt's sandbox-init -- once they run, there is deliberately no function anywhere in the library to widen the sandbox again. You can only ever have granted too little. Hold on to that word: irreversible. It is the guarantee -- and, as we will see, it is also the bill.
One apply call -- but underneath, two genuinely different kernels, and this is where a tour would stop and a review keeps going.

On Linux, Landlock. It is strictly allow-list: it cannot even express the idea deny this one path inside an otherwise allowed directory. On macOS, Seatbelt can. So the very shape of a policy differs by platform -- deny rules and symlink exceptions are macOS-only. That asymmetry is permanent, and it is a standing tax on every change.

Landlock is also versioned. nono probes the kernel from ABI version six down to version one, taking the highest it finds. The features depend on what it gets: rename control, truncation, device filtering -- and, critically, TCP network filtering, which needs version four or newer.

And here is a failure mode dressed as a feature. On an older kernel below version four, Landlock cannot filter the network at all. nono does not pretend otherwise -- it falls back to a coarser seccomp filter that can only block all networking, or force everything through the proxy. There is no partial host allow-list on those kernels. The tool tells you that, rather than silently giving you less than you asked for.

Fail-secure is the doctrine here, and it is enforced in the build itself: unwrap is denied workspace-wide, panics abort the process, and a configuration that fails to load is fatal -- never a quiet downgrade to no protection.

scenes.heading: A profile becomes a sandboxed agent

beats.narration: Watch a real invocation: nono run, with a profile, wrapping an agent like Claude Code.

First the profile is resolved and composed. Profiles can extend other profiles -- inheritance up to ten deep, with cycle detection -- folding builtin defaults, packs, and your overrides into one set of named policy groups.

The CLI resolves those groups into a concrete CapabilitySet -- the exact paths, hosts, and sockets -- and hands it to the supervisor. Every grant still carries where it came from: a user flag, the profile, a named group.

Now the pivotal step. The default strategy is supervised: the supervisor forks, and sandboxes only the child. The parent stays unsandboxed, so it can supervise, diagnose, and roll back. But forking is fragile -- the child allocates memory just after the fork, so if another thread held the allocator lock it would deadlock. nono refuses to fork with extra threads unless it knows they are idle keyring or crypto workers.

Inside the child, ordering is everything. Landlock is applied first; only then the seccomp filter. Reverse them and Landlock's own file opens get trapped by seccomp -- and the process deadlocks before it ever starts.

Then the child execs the agent. From this instant it is sealed: the capability set can only ever shrink. If a path was missed, the run fails -- and nono prints a diagnostic footer naming the probable missing grant and the exact flag to add.
And for the network, the agent reaches nothing directly. Every connection it is allowed to make goes to one place: the proxy.

nodes.label: OS keyring
nodes.sub: the real API key lives here

beats.narration: The proxy exists to answer one hard question: how do you let an agent call an authenticated API without ever letting the agent hold the key?

The answer is a phantom token. The agent is given a fake credential -- a 256-bit session token, random, checked in constant time. It never sees the real key at all. The real key is loaded from the OS keyring straight into the proxy, which lives outside the sandbox.

When a request passes through, the proxy verifies the phantom token, swaps in the real credential, and only then forwards it to an allow-listed host. To inspect HTTPS it has to terminate TLS itself -- a deliberate man-in-the-middle, using a throwaway certificate authority that lives just 24 hours.

It also hard-denies the things an allow-list might miss. The cloud metadata endpoint -- the link-local address that hands out instance credentials -- is blocked unconditionally, allow-list or not. And because hostnames can be re-pointed after a check, the proxy connects to the exact IP it resolved and verified, closing the door on DNS rebinding.

It is genuinely clever -- and it is also the thing to be most uneasy about. This proxy is unsandboxed, and it sees every credential and every decrypted byte. In a tool whose whole pitch is shrinking what you must trust, the proxy is the largest thing you still have to.

nodes.label: The proxy is trusted
nodes.sub: an unsandboxed MITM sees every key

nodes.label: Allow-list
nodes.sub: fails closed — the unforeseen is denied

nodes.label: Deny-list
nodes.sub: fails open — easier, but misses the unknown

beats.narration: Every architecture buys something and pays for it. Here is nono's bill, the part a tour leaves out.

First, irreversibility. It is the guarantee -- but it cuts both ways. Grant too little, and the sandbox cannot be widened for a running process; the agent simply fails, mid-task. That is the most likely 3am page. The diagnostic footer softens it, but the loop is real: it fails, you amend the profile, you re-run.

Second, the proxy. To keep credentials out of the agent, nono stands up a privileged, unsandboxed man-in-the-middle. A bug there is not a contained failure -- it is a credential disclosure. And it breaks any agent that pins its own certificates: the intercepted handshake fails hard, with no fallback.

Third, divergence. Linux and macOS will never enforce policy quite the same way -- so every security change has to be reasoned through twice, and tested on both, or a gap opens silently on one platform.

And underneath all of it, the founding trade-off. nono chose an allow-list over a deny-list. A deny-list is far easier to author -- block the bad, allow the rest -- but it fails open: anything unforeseen sails through. An allow-list fails closed; the unforeseen is denied by default. nono pays for that safety in authoring labour -- and that labour is the real risk, because a profile too painful to write is a profile a user will widen until the sandbox is just theatre.

scenes.heading: nono — an honest scorecard
points: Strong — isolation is kernel-enforced and irreversible: real, not advisory.
Strong — mechanism in the library, policy in the CLI: one primitive, every binding.
Costly — the allow-list tax; an under-grant fails the run, and the sandbox cannot widen.
Fragile — the proxy is a privileged, unsandboxed man-in-the-middle that sees every credential.
Skip it — for code you already fully trust, or where only a true VM boundary will do.

beats.narration: The honest scorecard. In its favour: the isolation is real. It is kernel-enforced and irreversible -- not an advisory wrapper an agent can talk its way out of.
And the library-versus-CLI split is the right seam: one pure primitive, policy held in one place, reused by every language binding.
Against it: the allow-list tax is unavoidable. Because the sandbox cannot widen once applied, an under-grant fails the run -- and convenience pressure will push profiles toward being too broad.

And the sharpest fragility: the proxy. It is a privileged, unsandboxed man-in-the-middle, and it is the one component that sees every credential -- the largest piece of trust in a tool built to reduce trust.

So the verdict. This is a sound, security-first architecture, honestly built -- the doctrine is in the code, not just the README. But it is not the right tool for code you already fully trust, nor a substitute where only a real VM boundary will do. Used for what it is -- a tight, kernel-enforced leash on a capable agent -- it earns its keep. That is the trade, named honestly.
